description: FTP transmits credentials and data in plaintext, making it vulnerable to interception and credential theft
Telnet provides unencrypted remote access, exposing all session data including passwords to network eavesdropping
SMB has a history of critical remote code execution vulnerabilities and should not be exposed publicly
Unencrypted HTTP exposes web traffic to interception and man-in-the-middle attacks
HTTPS is generally secure but misconfigured TLS or outdated certificates can introduce risk
SSH is relatively secure but weak credentials or outdated versions can allow unauthorized remote access
RDP exposed to the network is a common attack vector for brute force and remote exploitation
Exposing MySQL publicly risks unauthorized database access and potential data exfiltration
Open DNS resolvers can be abused for amplification attacks and DNS cache poisoning
Open or misconfigured SMTP servers can be exploited as spam relays or for user enumeration
